coredata.dc:title: Enhanced performance of self-powered ZnO-based PEC type UV photodetectors by loading GQDs to construct heterojunctions
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Meixian
Liu, Jun
Zhang, Tao
Liang, Nina
Yan, Lijuan
Lv, Fengzhen
Kong, Wenjie
Liu, Fuchi
Long, Lizhen
coredata.dc:description: The construction of semiconductor heterojunctions is an effective approach to enhance the performance of optoelectronic devices. In this study, graphene-quantum-dots (GQDs) were grown on ZnO nanorods arrays in-situ to form intimate heterojunctions. The fabricated photoelectrochemical (PEC) photodetector based on ZnO/GQDs heterojunctions displayed good self-powering characteristics, with an on/off current ratio of approximately 21 at zero bias, which was almost seven-fold higher than that of the bare ZnO device. The responsivity and detectivity of the ZnO/GQDs-based photodetector were also enhanced compared with those of bare ZnO devices. A unique type-II band alignment was established at the ZnO/GQDs heterojunction interface, which facilitated the carrier transfer. Moreover, the ZnO/GQDs heterojunction reduced the passivation of the surface states, resulting in large energy-band bending and an internal electric field at the semiconductor/electrolyte interface, which provided a powerful driving force for the separation and transport of photogenerated carriers. These combined effects enhance the performance of the photodetector, indicating that this approach can be used to fabricate high-performance PEC optoelectronic devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photodetector
Photoelectrochemical
Heterojunction
Surface state
Semiconductor/electrolyte interface

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi (No. 2020GXNSFBA297064 ), Science and Technology Major Project of Guangxi (No. Guike AA21077012-1 ), the Central Government Guidance Funds for Local Scientific and Technological Development of China (No. Guike ZY22096024 ), Open Project of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Nuclear Physics and Nuclear Technology (No. NLK2022-06 ), the National Science Foundation of China (No. 12104106 ), and the Innovative Entrepreneurial Training Program for College Students (Guangxi Normal University, No. 202210602050 , 202110602053 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The structure of ZnO/GQDs-based PEC photodetector.

SEM images of (a) ZnO and (b) ZnO/GQDs (insets: the cross-sectional images). (c) XRD patterns of ITO, ZnO and ZnO/GQDs. (d) HRTEM image of ZnO/GQDs. (e) TEM image of ZnO/GQDs and the corresponding EDS mapping of Zn, O, C. (f) Raman spectra of ZnO and ZnO/GQDs.
(a) Survey XPS spectra of ZnO/GQDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of ZnO/GQDs: (b) Zn 2p, (c) C 1 s, and (d) O 1 s.

(a) J-V characteristics and photocurrent density (the inset) of ZnO- and ZnO/GQDs-based photodetectors without and with 380 nm illumination. (b) Current density of the PEC photodetector at bias from 0 to 1.0 V. (c) Working mechanism of the PEC photodetector based on n-type of semiconductor.

Photoresponse behaviour of (a) ZnO and (b) ZnO/GQDs under light power density from 0 to 280 μW/cm2 at zero bias. (c) The dependence of I ph on light power density, and the corresponding (d) photoresponsivity and (e) specific detectivity at different light power density. (f) Time-resolved photoresponse under 380 nm periodic irradiation with 149 μW/cm2 at zero bias (inset is the partial enlarged image).
(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra, (b) the corresponding Tauc plot ((αhυ)2 vs. hυ), (c) PL spectra, and (d) EIS Nyquist curves of ZnO and ZnO/GQDs.

The M-S curves of (a) ZnO and (b) GQDs. (c) Schematic diagram of energy levels of ITO, ZnO, GQDs, Pt and redox potential of OH−/OH· couples. (d) The separation and transfer of photogenerated charge carriers in ZnO/GQDs-based PEC detector.

(a) Semi-logarithmic J-V plots of ZnO- and ZnO/GQDs-based PEC photodetectors. (b) Surface state mediated charge separation and transport at ZnO/electrolyte interface. (c) No surface state mediated charge separation and transport at ZnO/GQDs/electrolyte interface.

head.ce:title: Enhanced performance of self-powered ZnO-based PEC type UV photodetectors by loading GQDs to construct heterojunctions

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Physical Science and Technology, Guangxi Normal University, Guilin 541004, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physical Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The construction of semiconductor heterojunctions is an effective approach to enhance the performance of optoelectronic devices. In this study, graphene-quantum-dots (GQDs) were grown on ZnO nanorods arrays in-situ to form intimate heterojunctions. The fabricated photoelectrochemical (PEC) photodetector based on ZnO/GQDs heterojunctions displayed good self-powering characteristics, with an on/off current ratio of approximately 21 at zero bias, which was almost seven-fold higher than that of the bare ZnO device. The responsivity and detectivity of the ZnO/GQDs-based photodetector were also enhanced compared with those of bare ZnO devices. A unique type-II band alignment was established at the ZnO/GQDs heterojunction interface, which facilitated the carrier transfer. Moreover, the ZnO/GQDs heterojunction reduced the passivation of the surface states, resulting in large energy-band bending and an internal electric field at the semiconductor/electrolyte interface, which provided a powerful driving force for the separation and transport of photogenerated carriers. These combined effects enhance the performance of the photodetector, indicating that this approach can be used to fabricate high-performance PEC optoelectronic devices.

The ZnO/GQDs heterojunctions effectively reduce the passivation of the surface states, resulting in large energy-band bending and an internal electric field at the semiconductor/electrolyte interface.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of information technology, optoelectronic devices and sensors with high sensitivity, sustainability, energy efficiency, and eco-friendliness have become increasingly important [1]. Photodetectors, one of the most important members of various sensors, are employed for sensing optical signals. Ultraviolet (UV) photodetectors, which are monitoring systems for UV radiation, play a significant role in advanced communication, biochemical analysis, environmental monitoring, remote control, and flame sensing, among other fields [2–4]. Wide-bandgap semiconductors, such as nitrides (GaN, AlN, BN, etc.), carbides (SiC, diamond, etc.), and oxides (Ga2O3, ZnO, TiO2, etc.), and their composites are the most frequently used photoresponse materials for UV photodetectors [5]. At present, UV photodetectors are focused on heterojunction and field-effect transistor (FET) optoelectronic devices, which have various structures, including p-n junctions [6,7], p-i-n junctions [8–10], Schottky barriers [11,12], metal-insulator-semiconductor [13–15], and metal-semiconductor-metal [16–18]. However, the fabrication of these devices requires single-crystal substrates and rigid epitaxial processes, resulting in a high production costs.

Recently, photoelectrochemical (PEC) photodetectors have garnered considerable attention owing to their simple manufacturing process, low cost, environmental friendliness, and the presence of abundant raw materials [19–21]. Furthermore, self-powered devices can be easily achieved in PEC-type photodetectors owing to the unique interfacial electric field at the semiconductor/electrolyte interface. A typical PEC-type UV photodetector comprise three important components: a wide-bandgap semiconductor (photoanode), counter electrode, and an electrolyte. Currently, platinum (Pt) is the most frequently used counter electrode in electrochemical systems owing to its good catalytic activity and stability. The electrolyte provides ionic conductivity, which transports ions between the photoanode and counter electrode. Notably, H2O can also be employed as an electrolyte in PEC-type photodetectors, rendering these devices green, safe, and stable. Currently, TiO2 and ZnO are the most promising photoanodes for PEC-type UV photodetectors [22–24]. The band structure of ZnO is highly similar to that of TiO2; however, ZnO possesses higher electron mobility [25]. In particular, one-dimensional ZnO has been used as a photoanode in PEC-type UV photodetectors owing to its wide bandgap, high surface state density, and excellent electrical transport property [25–27]. However, the performance of the photodetectors based on bare ZnO photoanodes is still not satisfactory, mainly because of the fast recombination of charge carriers owing to the high exciton binding energy and capture of surface states [28–30].

Interface engineering by decorating semiconductor photoanodes with quantum-dots (QDs) is an effective approach for dissociating excitons and promoting carrier transport in various optoelectronic devices. Zhou et al. demonstrated that by decorating with CsPbBr3 QDs, the responsivity and detectivity of mesoporous-TiO2 photodetectors were enhanced from ∼2.2 to ∼24.5 AW−1 and 3.5 × 1012 to 8.9 × 1013 Jones, respectively [31]. Bi et al. reported that the photocurrent of a ZnO nanowire device increased from 0.167 to 1.84 mA after decoration with CdS QDs [32]. Wei et al. fabricated a photodetector based on the CdS@Cd x Zn1−
x S QD-decorated CsPbBr3 photoanode, and the responsivity improved by approximately 10 fold compared with that of pristine CsPbBr3 nanowire devices [33]. The environmental stability of photoresponsive materials is critical for their practical application in optoelectronic devices. Graphene-quantum-dots (GQDs) exhibit excellent photo-chemical stabilities [34,35] and strong charge-transfer abilities [36,37]. Moreover, graphene can be connected to semiconductor nanomaterials through functional groups to form intimate heterojunctions [38]. Therefore, we expected that GQDs modified ZnO will exhibit enhanced photoresponse properties. However, most reports on interface engineering have focused on the metal-semiconductor contacts or semiconductor-semiconductor junction devices [39–41]. PEC photodetectors involve complex photoelectrochemical processes, and the study of semiconductor/electrolyte interface is lacking. Herein, the in-situ growth of GQDs was employed to construct the intimate ZnO/GQDs heterojunctions. PEC-type UV photodetectors were assembled using ZnO/GQDs, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA)/H2O, and Pt/indium tin oxide (ITO) as the photoanode, quasi solid-state electrolyte, and counter electrode, respectively. The ZnO/GQDs-based photodetector displayed a better photoresponse performance than the bare ZnO device. A unique type-II band alignment was established at the ZnO/GQDs heterojunction interface, which promoted the separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers. Furthermore, the ZnO/GQDs heterojunctions reduced the passivation of the surface states, resulting in large energy-band bending and an internal electric field at the semiconductor/electrolyte interface, which provided a powerful driving force for separating and transporting charge carriers.

Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, 99.5 %), hexamethylenetetramine (HMTA, (CH2)6N4, 99.0 %), ethanolamine (C2H7NO, 99.0 %), ethyl alcohol (C2H5OH, 99.5 %), and acetone (CH3COCH3, 99.5 %) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., LTD. Citric acid (C6H8O7, 99.5 %), 2-methoxyethanol (C3H8O2, 99.5 %), and N, N-dimethylformamide (C3H7NO, 99.5 %) were purchased from Shanghai Maclean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. All the materials were used as received without further purification.

ITO glasses were used as the substrates. Prior to the growth of the ZnO nanorod arrays, ZnO seed layers were deposited on the ITO substrates by using a sol-gel spin-coating technique, followed by annealing. Subsequently, ZnO nanorod arrays were grown on the ITO substrates by using hydrothermal method. Finally, GQDs were grown on ZnO nanorod arrays in-situ using solvothermal method to form intimate heterojunctions. More details on the preparation of the ZnO nanorod arrays and GQDs are provided in the Supporting Information.

The morphology and elemental composition of the materials were characterized using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Helios G4 CX, Zeiss Auriga Inc., Germany) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Talos F200s, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) instruments with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). The crystallinity was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku MiniFlex 6600, Japan) with Cu Kα radiation. The chemical compositions were analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB250Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. USA), and the Raman spectra were obtained using a laser confocal micro-Raman system (LabRAM HR Evolution, HORIBA, France). Absorption spectra were recorded using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2700, Shimadzu, Japan) equipped with an integration sphere. The photoluminescence spectra were collected using a spectrofluorophotometer (FLS1000, Edinburgh Instruments, UK). The electrochemical tests were conducted on an electrochemical workstation (CHI 760E, CH Instruments Inc., USA) using a three-electrode system. The prepared films were used as working electrodes with an active area of 0.6 cm2. A Pt sheet and an Ag/AgCl electrode were used as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively. An aqueous solution of 0.1 M Na2SO4 served as the electrolyte, which was bubbled with N2 for 0.5 h before each test. The Mott-Schottky measurements were recorded at 1 KHz. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted at a frequency range of 0.01–106 Hz under zero bias.

As shown in Fig. 1 , our PEC photodetector comprises three important parts: the ZnO/GQDs photoanode, PVA/H2O solid-state electrolyte, and Pt/ITO counter electrode. The methods for preparing the PVA/H2O electrolyte and Pt/ITO counter electrode are provided in the Supporting Information. A Keithley 2602B SourceMeter was used to measure the photoresponse performance of the PEC photodetectors in air. The current density-voltage (J-V) curves were recorded by linear sweep voltammetry measurements from −1 to 1 V with a scanning rate of 10 mV s−1. Amperometric current-time (I-t) curves were collected from 0 to 1 V with a sampling interval of 10 s. UV lights (380 nm) with light intensities ranging from 0 to 275 μW cm−2 were employed to illuminate the photoanode.

Fig. 2a shows the top and cross-sectional views of the ZnO nanorod arrays. Hexagonal ZnO nanorods grew nearly vertically and uniformly on the ITO substrate. Gaps were observed between the ZnO nanorod crystals. The loosely packed structure is favorable for contact between the ZnO nanorods and electrolyte. Fig. 2b shows the top and cross-sectional SEM images of the ZnO nanorods after the in-situ growth of GQDs. No significant difference was observed between the morphologies of the top-sectional SEM images of ZnO and ZnO/GQDs. EDS analysis (Fig. S1) indicated that the atomic percentage of C in ZnO/GQDs was 1.45 %. Fig. 2c shows the XRD patterns of ZnO and ZnO/GQDs. The characteristic peaks of the (100), (002), (101), (102), and (103) planes at 31.7º, 34.4º, 36.2º, 47.6º, and 62.9º, respectively, indicate that the ZnO nanorods had a hexagonal wurtzite structure (p63mc, PDF card No. 36–1451). The sharp diffraction peak of (002) indicates that the ZnO nanorods grew along the c-axis-preferred orientation of the ITO substrate. Additionally, a weak characteristic graphene diffraction peak of (101) at 20.62º can be observed in the enlarged XRD pattern of the ZnO/GQDs (Fig. S2). As indicated by the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) of ZnO/GQDs (Fig. 2d), the lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.26 nm are ascribed to the (002) plane of the ZnO nanorod, whereas the lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.22 nm are attributed to the (100) crystal phase of graphite. These results demonstrate that GQDs with particle size of 2–3 nm were successfully decorated on the ZnO nanorods. As shown in the TEM-EDS mapping of the ZnO/GQDs (Fig. 2e), Zn and O originating from the ZnO nanorods were uniformly distributed. The strong signal of the C element mainly originated the TEM grid, but the relatively weaker C signal in the ZnO nanorod region belongs to the GQDs.

The presence of GQDs was further confirmed by Raman spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2f, the Raman spectra of pure ZnO and ZnO/GQDs exhibited the typical vibrational modes of the hexagonal wurtzite structure of ZnO. Based on the group theory, the optical-phonon modes at the first Brillouin zone center (Γ point) can be expressed by the irreducible representation: Γopt = A1 + E1 + 2E2 + 2B1 [42]. The A1 and E1 modes are polar modes and are both Raman and infrared active, and the B1 modes are silent modes, whereas the remaining E2 modes are nonpolar and Raman active. The typical vibrational modes of the hexagonal wurtzite structure of ZnO were observed for both samples. The strongest peak at 437 cm−1 is attributed to the nonpolar optical mode of ZnO [E2 High], whereas the peaks at 573 and 1147 cm−1 are assigned to the polar optical mode of ZnO, associated with transverse optical (TO) and longitudinal optical (LO) phonons, respectively. Two weak peaks centered at 1455 and 1598 cm−1 are observed in the Raman spectrum of ZnO/GQDs, which can be attributed to the well-ordered graphite (G-band) and disordered sp2 carbon (D-band), respectively [43,44].

XPS was employed to analyze the bonding configuration and surface chemical states of the ZnO/GQDs. The survey spectra in Fig. 3a show the presence of Zn, C, and O in both samples. Fig. 3b shows the typical Zn 2p core levels (Zn 2P3/2 and 2P1/2), with peaks at 1022.3 and 1045.2 eV, and the observed spin-orbit splitting falls within 23.0 (±0.1) eV, which is consistent with the results of a previous study [45]. As shown in Fig. 3c, the C spectrum was deconvoluted into three characteristic peaks centered at CI (284.4 eV) for C–C bonding, CII (286.2 eV) for C–O bonding, and CIII (288.5 eV) for O–CO bonding [46]. The C–C bond represents the graphitized sp2 carbon. The other two types of bonding, namely, C–O and O–CO, indicate the existence of a large number of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of the GQDs. The O 1 s spectra of the ZnO/GQDs (Fig. 3d) were carefully deconvoluted into three peaks (OI, OII, and OIII) to examine the detailed oxygen states [47]. The OI peak at 530 ± 0.3 eV is attributed to the O2− ions of the hexagonal Zn2+ ion array. The OII peak at 531.4 ± 0.3 eV is associated with the O2− ions in the oxygen-deficient regions within the ZnO matrix. The OIII peak at 532.3 ± 0.3 eV is usually attributed to the oxygen-containing groups on the surface of ZnO, such as –OH, –CO3, and chemically adsorbed O2. For a better comparison, the percentages of OI, OII, and OIII were calculated using the relative integrated intensities of the three fitting peaks, namely, 1.86, 26.16 and 71.98 %, respectively. These results further confirm that the GQDs were decorated on the ZnO nanorods.

To investigate the optical detection properties of the PEC-type UV photodetectors, the curves of current density versus voltage (J-V) were measured with and without 380-nm light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 4a , the dark current curves of the PEC photodetectors based on the ZnO and ZnO/GQDs photoanodes exhibit rectification characteristics. The photocurrent density of a ZnO/GQDs-based PEC photodetector, defined as J ph = J light−J dark (J dark represents the current density without light illumination, J light represents the current density with UV-light illumination), is significantly higher than that of a ZnO-based device (inset in Fig. 4a). Notably, the current response at zero bias indicates the self-powered behavior of these photodetectors (see Fig. S3). The J ph values are approximately 0.08 and 2.12 μA/cm2 for the ZnO and ZnO/GQDs-based photodetectors, respectively, under illumination at 380 nm and bias of 0 V. The calculated on/off ratios of J light/J dark were approximately 3 and 21 for the ZnO- and ZnO/GQDs-based devices, respectively. These results imply that the loading of GQDs onto the ZnO nanorods can significantly increase their photocurrent values. As shown in Fig. 4b, the current can be regulated by an external bias, which linearly increases with increasing bias from 0 to 1 V.

This self-powered behavior can be explained as follows (Fig. 4c): in generally, donor-type defects are easily formed in ZnO semiconductors, which can transfer electrons to the conduction band and contribute to the unintentional n-type conductivity [48]. The initial Fermi level of an n-type semiconductor is more negative than that of the electrolyte (the redox potential of the redox couples in the electrolyte). Thus, when an n-type semiconductor is in contact with the electrolyte, electrons are transferred from the semiconductor to the electrolyte until their Fermi levels are aligned. After equilibrium, a positive space-charge layer is established in the semiconductor to prevent the further flow of electrons into the electrolyte, causing the energy band edges to bend upward. The working mechanism of a PEC photodetector based on an n-type semiconductor is described as follows:
(1)
S + hv →
h
VB
+
+
e
CB
−
(2)
2
H 2
O ↔
H 3
O
+
+ O
H
−
(3)
h
+
+ O
H
−
→ OH ·
(4)
e
−
+ OH ·
→ O
H
−

Under UV light irradiation, an n-type semiconductor (S) absorbs photons to generate electrons (
e
CB
−
) in the conduction band (CB), leaving behind holes (
h
VB
+
) in the valence band (VB). Pure water (H2O) is a weak electrolyte that dissociates into hydronium (H3O+) and hydroxide (OH−) ions. The positive space charge layer drives the migrating of photogenerated holes to the semiconductor/electrolyte interface, and subsequently oxidizes OH− to produce hydroxyl radical (OH·). Simultaneously, the photogenerated electrons move to the ITO electrode, where they are extracted by the external circuit and reduce OH· to OH− at the Pt counter electrode. During this cycling, the internal electric field in the space charge layer acts as an “engine” to drive the separation and transport of the carriers. Therefore, this PEC-type photodetector can be directly powered by UV-light illumination and can operate without an external bias.

To further investigate the optical response behavior, the dependence of the dynamic photoresponse on the irradiation power (P) was characterized under irradiation at 380 nm with various light power densities at zero bias. As shown in Fig. 5 a and b, the photoresponse switching performances of ZnO and ZnO/GQDs are reproducible without obvious current decay, indicating that the PEC devices can operate stably. The dependence of the photocurrent on the light intensity can be expressed by the power law [49]:
(5)
I = A
P
θ
where A is a constant for a given wavelength, P is the light intensity, and θ is the exponent determined by the photocurrent response to light intensity. The dependence of I ph on the light intensity is shown plot in Fig. 5c, which was fitted using Eq. (5). The strong sublinear dependence of I ph on light intensity can be attributed to the saturation of traps or an acceleration of detrapping dynamics with increasing carrier density [50]. The θ values in the curves of ZnO and ZnO/GQDs were calculated as approximately 0.66 and 0.68, respectively, implying the low trap state density.

Photoresponsivity (Rλ ) and detectivity (D*) are key variables to evaluate the sensitivity of detectors, and can be calculated as follows:
(6)
R λ
=
I
p h
P S
(7)
D
*
=
R λ
S
1 / 2
/
(
2 q
I
d a r k
)
1 / 2
where q is the electron charge, S is the effective illumination area, and P is power density. The responsivity (Fig. 5d) and detectivity (Fig. 5e) of the ZnO/GQDs-based PEC detector improved by approximately about 10 fold and more than 2 fold compared with those of the pristine ZnO-based device. In particular, the exponential decay relationships are observed from the Rλ versus P and D* versus P curves for both detectors, indicating that the photodetectors have more potential in the lower P regions. The Rλ and D* values decrease with increasing P, indicating that the photogenerated carriers can be trapped by the deep defects [50]. The cycle stability was measured under periodic irradiation at 380 nm with a power density of 149 μW/cm2 at zero bias. As shown in Fig. 5f, after 225 cycles, the J ph values still maintained at 84.8 and 86.8 % of the initial values for the ZnO- and ZnO/GQDs-based PEC detectors, respectively. The ZnO/GQDs-based photodetector exhibited better photoresponse performance than the ZnO-based device.

In general, the performance of junction-based photodetectors is governed by three processes: (i) the generation of photogenerated carriers in the semiconductor by light irradiation; (ii) the separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers; and (iii) the injection of minority carriers from the electrodes to the redox couple in the electrolyte. To determine the reason for the enhanced photoresponse performance after incorporation of GQDs into ZnO, we first studied the light absorption properties. As shown in Fig. 6a , both the bare ZnO and ZnO/GQDs exhibited a strong UV response with obvious edge absorption at approximately 380 nm. The UV response was ascribed to the intrinsic band-gap of ZnO [47]. The UV absorption intensity decreased after decoration with GQDs, which probably originated from the enhanced scattering caused by the change in the microscopic surface. The spectrum of pure GQDs exhibited three typical absorption bands (Fig. S4) that can be attributed to the π→π* transition absorption of CC, n→π* transition absorption of CO, and bandgap transition absorption, respectively [51]. No significant change was observed in visible absorption after the incorporation of GQDs into the ZnO nanorods, which may be related to the weak visible absorption and small loading amount of the GQDs. These results suggested that the promoted photoresponse performance of ZnO/GQDs is independent of light absorption. Since ZnO is a direct-gap semiconductor, the bandgap can be estimated by the Tauc equation (8) [52]:
(8)
α h υ = B
(
h υ −
E g
)
1 / 2
where α, h, υ, E g and B are the absorption coefficient, Planck constant, light frequency, bandgap energy, and a constant, respectively. Fig. 6b shows the plots of (αhυ)2 versus hυ and the bandgap energy (E g) can be obtained by extending the tangent segment to the x-axis, which is approximately 3.22 eV for both pure ZnO and ZnO/GQDs. Additionally, the E g value for pure GQDs is approximately 2.70 eV, as shown in Fig. S5.

Photoluminescence (PL) emission reflects the combination of photogenerated electrons and holes; for example, a higher PL intensity corresponds to faster recombination [52]. As shown in Fig. 6c, both samples show a broad emission in the range of 390–650 nm with four clear peaks centered at 450, 467, 481, and 492 nm. The PL intensity of ZnO/GQDs is lower reduced than that of ZnO. Notably, the visible emission of ZnO is closely related to the deep level defects, such as oxygen vacancies [53,54]. The pure GQDs showed broad-band PL emission from 400 to 650 nm (Fig. S6). However, the PL emission was reduced by loading GQDs onto ZnO, implying means that the separation of the photogenerated electrons and holes increased. The separation and transfer of electrons and holes in photoelectrodes can also be characterized using EIS. As shown in Fig. 6d, the Nyquist plots of all samples exhibit a single circular arc in the medium-high frequency region, which corresponds to the charge-transfer resistance. The arc radius of ZnO/GQDs was smaller than that of ZnO, indicating that the charge-transfer resistance decreased after loading with GQDs owing to the formation of ZnO/GQDs heterojunctions.

To clarify the mechanism for the separation and transfer of electron/hole pairs, the band structures of the semiconductors were determined using Mott-Schottky (M-S) tests. The M-S curves of both pure ZnO and pure GQDs display a positive slope, indicating the characteristics of n-type semiconductors (Fig. 7a , b). The flat-band potentials of the semiconductors were determined using M-S Equation:
(9)
1
C
2
=
2
e
ε
r
ε
o
N D
A
2
(
E −
E
F B
−
k B
T
e
)
where C is the space-charge capacitance; e is the electronic charge; εr is the relative permittivity; εo is the vacuum permittivity; A is the active surface of electrode; ND is the carrier density of an n-type semiconductor; E is the applied potential; EFB is the flat-band potential; kB is the Boltzmann constant; and T is the thermodynamic temperature. In general, a part of kBT/e can be neglected. Thus, by extrapolating the linear portion to the x-axis, the EFB values are determined to be approximately −0.16 and −0.53 eV (vs. Ag/AgCl) for pure ZnO and pure GQDs, respectively. The relationship between the conduction band minimum (CBM) and valence band maximum (VBM) is as follows: E CBM = E VBM−E g. As shown in Figs. 6b and S5, the E g values are 3.22 and 2.70 eV for pure ZnO and pure GQDs, respectively. In general, the CBM of n-type semiconductors is more negative (0–0.3 eV) than E FB [55] . The difference between the CBM and E FB values was set as 0.2 eV. Thus, the band structure data of ZnO and GQDs on the electrochemical scale were calculated (Table S1). Moreover, the work functions of ITO and Pt are −4.7 eV [56] and – 5.65 eV [57] in the solid state scale, respectively. Fig. 7c and d show the band structure and schematic of the photogenerated carrier transfer in the ZnO/GQDs-based PEC detector. The incorporation of GQDs resulted in a unique type-II band alignment of the 1D-ZnO/0D-GQDs, which was beneficial for carrier transfer. Under UV-light irradiation, both ZnO and GQDs absorb light to generate electrons (
e
−
) and holes (
h
+
). The CBM level of GQDs is more negative than that of ZnO, resulting in a large energy difference to drive the injection of
e
−
from GQDs to ZnO. Meanwhile, the VBM level of ZnO is more positive than that of GQDs, transferring
h
+
from ZnO to GQDs and subsequently moving
h
+
to the active sites for oxidizing OH− to OH·. The photogenerated electrons move toward the ITO electrode, and are then extracted by the external circuit to reduce OH· to OH− at the Pt counter electrode.

The enhanced photoresponse performance of the PEC photodetector based on the ZnO/GQDs heterostructure can be explained by the semi-logarithmic J-V plots and energy band structures of ZnO and ZnO/GQDs (Fig. 8 ). As a type of electrochemical photovoltaic cell [58], the absolute value of the applied bias at the minimum current in the semi-logarithmic J-V plot is equal to the open-circuit voltage value (denoted as V open), which reflects the original width of the space-charge layer and degree of energy-band bending. As shown in Fig. 8a, for the ZnO-based PEC photodetector, the V open value is 0.84 V under dark conditions. Surface states, including surface lattice imperfections (such as oxygen vacancies, as shown by the XPS results in Fig. 6) and lattice fractures at the boundary of the periodic crystal lattice exist in ZnO nanorods, and contribute to energy-band bending at the ZnO/water interface [59]. For the ZnO/GQDs-based photodetector, the V open value is 0.74 V under dark conditions, which is lower than that of the ZnO-based PEC photodetector (0.84 V). The reduced energy-band bending can be attributed to the passivation of surface states by loading with GQDs. Notably, the band bending degree of the semiconductor/electrolyte interface in both the ZnO- and ZnO/GQDs-based PEC devices decreased under light irradiation, but the degree of band bending in the ZnO/GQDs device was much larger than that in the ZnO device. This can be explained by the different surface state passivation mechanisms of ZnO and ZnO/GQDs, as described below. Under UV-light illumination, the V open value of the ZnO photodetector greatly reduced to 0.12 V. Although a large number of photogenerated electrons and holes are generated by UV-light illumination, the inefficient transfer of charge carriers from ZnO to the electrolyte (Fig. 8b) resulted in the passivation of surface states by trapping these charge carriers, leading to small band bending. Alternatively, considering the ZnO/GQDs-based photodetector under UV-light illumination, the V open value is 0.56 V, which is much larger than that of the ZnO-based photodetector (0.12 V). As shown in Fig. 8c, the ZnO/GQDs heterojunction prevented the surface state passivation by promoting the transfer of photogenerated electrons and holes at the ZnO/GQDs interface. Thus, the unpassivated surface states contribute to large energy-band bending and a large internal electric field at the semiconductor/electrolyte interface, which provide a powerful driving force for the transport of photogenerated charge carriers. In particular, the ZnO/GQDs heterojunction not only promoted the separation and transfer of photogenerated charge carriers, but also prevented the passivation of surface states, leading to a large energy-band bending at the semiconductor/electrolyte interface. These combined effects enhance the photoresponse performance of the ZnO/GQDs-based PEC photodetector.

In this study, we successfully decorated ZnO nanorod arrays with GQDs by using a solvothermal method to produce intimate ZnO/GQDs heterojunctions. The ZnO/GQDs heterojunction was used as the photoanode to assemble a PEC-type UV photodetector. The prepared ZnO/GQDs photodetector displayed an on/off current ratio of approximately 21 under UV illumination at a bias of 0 V, which was almost 7 fold higher than that of the bare ZnO device (approximately 3). The responsivity and detectivity of the PEC-detector were enhanced by 10 and 2 fold, respectively, compared with those of pristine ZnO devices. Moreover, self-powered PEC photodetectors exhibited good stability under UV irradiation. The type-II band structure of the ZnO/GQDs heterojunction was determined using M-S analysis, which promoted the separation and transfer of the photogenerated charge carriers. Moreover, the ZnO/GQDs heterojunction reduced the capture of photogenerated carriers through surface states. This resulted in large energy-band bending and an internal electric field at the semiconductor/electrolyte interface, which provided a large driving force for the transfer of photogenerated charge carriers. These combined effects enhanced the performance of the photodetector, indicating that this approach can be used to fabricate high-performance PEC optoelectronic devices.

Meixian Li: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Jun Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Project administration. Tao Zhang: Investigation, Validation. Nina Liang: Validation, Visualization. Lijuan Yan: Methodology, Resources. Fengzhen Lv: Methodology, Supervision. Wenjie Kong: Methodology, Supervision. Fuchi Liu: Methodology, Resources, Supervision. Lizhen Long: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this work.
